segments.text: Thirty years. That's how long tinnitus researchers had essentially the same toolkit. Sound masking. Tricyclic antidepressants used off-label. A protocol from 1995 called Tinnitus Retraining Therapy. And 25 million Americans with nowhere scientifically credible to go.
Then March 2023. The FDA granted De Novo Class II authorization to a device called Lenire — the first bimodal neuromodulation system ever cleared for tinnitus.
That same 18-month window saw internet-delivered CBT trials report results matching in-person therapy. The field moved more in one and a half years than in the prior decade combined.
We're going to map exactly where that frontier stands now. What holds up. What never held up but is still being sold. And what's promising but not ready to recommend.
One framing issue upfront — because it matters for everything that follows. Tinnitus is not a disease. It's a symptom. The auditory system producing a phantom signal.
Its causes are heterogeneous: noise-induced cochlear damage, age-related hearing loss, ototoxic medications, vascular anomalies. That heterogeneity is why a single tinnitus cure has remained elusive.
You can't treat a symptom with one intervention when the underlying mechanisms differ patient to patient.
Exactly. And that structural reality is the first thing most tinnitus product marketing fails to disclose.
Start with the scale. How many people actually have this?

A 2022 systematic review and meta-analysis in JAMA Neurology — the most comprehensive global estimate to date — found that tinnitus affects 14.4% of the adult population worldwide. Approximately 740 million adults.

Haider and colleagues wrote: "The pooled prevalence of any tinnitus among adults was 14.4%, while the pooled prevalence of severe tinnitus was 2.3%." In the United States alone, 25 million Americans report tinnitus lasting five or more consecutive minutes in the past year. Direct economic costs: an estimated $26 billion annually.
And this is a population that's been largely handed supplements and hope for three decades.
That's the honest summary. Which makes the 2023 inflection point significant — and worth scrutinizing carefully.
So let's get into Lenire. What is it, and what does the evidence actually show?

Lenire is a bimodal neuromodulation device developed by Neuromod Devices in Dublin, Ireland. It delivers synchronized sound to the ears and mild electrical stimulation to the tongue via a mouthpiece — simultaneously.
The hypothesis: pairing auditory and somatosensory input triggers neuroplasticity in the auditory cortex, gradually reducing the brain's representation of the tinnitus signal.
The brain's representation — not the tinnitus itself.

Right. That distinction matters. The evidence trail runs through three controlled trials. TENT-A1, 2020 — Brian Conlon at Beaumont Hospital Dublin enrolled 326 participants. After 12 weeks, 86.2% of treatment-compliant participants showed improvement on the Tinnitus Handicap Inventory. 80.1% maintained that improvement at 12-month follow-up.
That's a strong-sounding number. But TENT-A1 didn't have a sham control arm.

Correct — the authors acknowledged that limitation. Conlon et al. wrote: "86.2% of treatment-compliant participants had improvements in THI score at end of treatment." Without a sham arm, you can't fully separate the device effect from expectation and the passage of time.
TENT-A2 in 2022 was larger — 298 participants, randomized. Average THI reduction across both arms: 18.5 points at 12 weeks. At 12-month follow-up without further treatment, that held at 20.2 points.
What's the minimum clinically important difference on that scale?

Six to seven points. Conlon and colleagues wrote: "There was an average reduction across both arms of 18.5 points for THI... improvements were sustained for 12 months after treatment was withdrawn." An 18 to 20 point reduction is substantial for this field.

Then TENT-A3 in 2024 — the pivotal trial that preceded FDA authorization. 112 participants across three independent sites. This time with an active control: six weeks of bimodal stimulation against six weeks of sound therapy alone. The bimodal arm outperformed. 79.4% of patients experienced clinically significant improvement.
But the FDA authorization itself is careful about what it claims.

Very. The FDA CDRH De Novo Authorization reads: "Lenire is intended to provide bimodal stimulation to temporarily relieve the symptoms of tinnitus in patients 18 years of age and older suffering from at least moderate tinnitus." Temporarily. That's a word the device's marketing sometimes understates.
And De Novo — that's not the same as a full FDA approval.

It isn't. De Novo establishes a new Class II device category for novel technologies without a direct predicate. FDA reviewed the evidence and found it sufficient for clearance — but it is not the gold standard of a Phase III pharmaceutical approval.
The evidence base for Lenire is real and relatively robust for this field. It should not be inflated into something the regulatory process did not claim.
Now — the device gets attention. But you might argue the bigger practical story is what happened with CBT.

I would argue that. The most consequential shift in tinnitus care may be access. CBT addresses the 90% of sufferers who have no realistic path to a $3,000 to $5,000 device or a specialist audiologist.
Walk through the Beukes trial.

Eldré Beukes at Anglia Ruskin University ran the largest program of internet-delivered CBT for tinnitus in any English-language setting. The 2018 randomized clinical trial in JAMA Otolaryngology compared guided iCBT against face-to-face clinical care in 102 participants.

Beukes and colleagues wrote: "Guided internet-based CBT was non-inferior to face-to-face clinical care for treatment of tinnitus; both produced comparable reductions in tinnitus distress." On insomnia outcomes, iCBT actually outperformed the in-person arm.
Non-inferior. Not worse. And UK waiting lists for in-person specialist care average 18 months. In the United States, trained audiologists are concentrated in major metropolitan areas.

Which makes non-inferiority a finding with real clinical weight, not just statistical interest. A 2024 meta-analysis covering 21 RCTs and 1,847 participants confirmed the direction of effect. Roos and colleagues: "Internet-based CBT and mindfulness interventions for tinnitus consistently outperformed waitlist controls on tinnitus distress and associated mental health outcomes."
Now — there's a distinction that gets buried in every piece of tinnitus coverage. CBT works. But it doesn't work the way most sufferers want it to work.

This is the distinction that's everything. Henrik Hesser at Linköping University in Sweden — 15 studies, 1,091 participants. For tinnitus distress specifically, CBT versus passive control yielded a Hedges's g of 0.70.

Hesser and colleagues wrote: "CBT compared with passive control yielded a statistically significant mean effect size for tinnitus-specific measures, Hedges's g = 0.70." That's a medium-to-large effect.
That's distress. Not loudness.

The meta-analytic literature is consistent on this point. CBT does not reduce how loud patients perceive their tinnitus. It reduces how much that perception controls their life — the grip on attention, sleep, and mood.
For people with severe tinnitus-related handicap, that functional improvement can be transformative. But it should be explained accurately, not sold as something it isn't.
Most sufferers come in wanting the ringing to stop.

And what CBT delivers is a different outcome. That needs to be said before someone begins treatment. The Cochrane review on CBT for tinnitus in 2020 confirmed: low-to-moderate certainty evidence that CBT reduces the negative impact of tinnitus on quality of life. No effect on loudness.
What about TRT? Tinnitus Retraining Therapy has been around since the mid-nineties.

TRT combines directive counseling with sound enrichment — low-level broadband noise. The Cochrane TRT review found limited evidence of efficacy: a single included study showing benefit, rated low quality.

A 2021 meta-analysis of 13 RCTs found TRT improved outcomes when added to medical treatment, but the underlying evidence was rated low quality with high risk of bias. TRT may help. The evidence that it outperforms well-delivered CBT is not there.
Now the supplement shelf. Because that's where most people in distress actually land first.
Lipo-Flavonoid has been marketed for tinnitus in the United States for more than 60 years. In that time, one adequately designed randomized controlled trial examined it.

Le and colleagues in Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery wrote: "We were unable to conclude that either manganese or Lipoflavonoid Plus is an effective treatment for tinnitus based on this randomized controlled trial." Sixty years of marketing. One failed trial.
The American Academy of Otolaryngology doesn't recommend it. The American Tinnitus Association doesn't recommend it. It continues to be sold in pharmacies throughout the United States.

The broader category — Tinnitus 911, Ring Ease, TruSound, ginkgo biloba formulations — same pattern. Park and Moon in Otolaryngology wrote: "No studied over-the-counter tinnitus product has demonstrated replicable, long-term reduction of tinnitus impact in excess of placebo effects."

Ginkgo biloba: no clear benefit across adequately powered studies. The European Food Safety Authority has banned health claims for lemon bioflavonoids within the EU. The U.S. market faces no equivalent restriction.
Why? What keeps these products on the shelf?
Regulatory structure. Supplements are regulated as food, not drugs. They require no proof of efficacy before sale. The tinnitus supplement market is not a conspiracy — it is a regulatory arbitrage.
Two approaches that are genuinely worth watching — but not ready for recommendation. VNS and rTMS.

Vagus nerve stimulation paired with tones was pioneered by Michael Kilgard and Navzer Engineer at the University of Texas at Dallas. The theoretical basis: surgically implanted VNS paired with tone presentations drives targeted cortical reorganization, potentially reversing the maladaptive plasticity that generates tinnitus.

The first human pilot — Tyler et al., 2017, Scientific Reports, 30 participants. Tyler and colleagues wrote: "50% of participants in the paired VNS group showed clinically meaningful improvements compared to 28% in controls." That's a real signal.
But the limitation is significant.

The approach requires surgical implantation. Non-invasive transcutaneous auricular VNS is being studied — a 2024 Frontiers in Neuroscience review called early evidence encouraging but not yet definitive. That's the honest characterization.
And rTMS — repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation?

Longer history, more ambiguous outcome. A 2022 meta-analysis of nine RCTs found rTMS produced modest improvement. But Tao and Fang wrote: "Active rTMS was no more effective than sham rTMS as a tinnitus treatment method in several well-controlled trials; inconsistency across studies prevents recommendation."
Coil placement, stimulus frequency, session count, patient selection — none of it is standardized enough to call any protocol standard care.
And pharmacology.

One frank sentence: no drug has ever received FDA approval for primary tinnitus. Antidepressants and anxiolytics may reduce the associated psychological burden — they do not reduce tinnitus itself. Acamprosate showed a signal in one trial. That is the field's best pharmacological result. It has not been replicated at scale.
So. What can we actually say.

We can say: Lenire is the first device to receive FDA De Novo Class II authorization for tinnitus — three controlled trials, average THI reductions of 18 to 28 points, substantially above the 6 to 7 point minimal clinically important difference. Effect durable at 12-month follow-up. No device-related serious adverse events.

We can say: internet-delivered CBT is non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress reduction. For 25 million Americans with limited access to specialist audiologists, this changes the practical options available.

We can say: CBT reduces tinnitus distress — Hedges's g of 0.70 versus passive control. It does not reduce tinnitus loudness. These are different outcomes. Both are real. Only one is consistently achievable.

We can say: there are no over-the-counter supplements with RCT-level evidence of efficacy for tinnitus. Not Lipo-Flavonoid. Not ginkgo biloba. Not any Tinnitus 911 formulation. Legal products. Not supported by controlled evidence.

What we can't say: that any current treatment cures tinnitus in the sense of eliminating the perception permanently in most patients. We can't say Lenire works equally across all tinnitus subtypes — the pivotal trials enrolled patients with moderate-to-severe THI, primarily noise-induced or age-related tinnitus.
We can't say that VNS-paired stimulation or any pre-clinical approach will translate to routine clinical use. Promising pilot results have a high failure rate in tinnitus research specifically.
And we can't say why 30 years of documented placebo-equivalent supplement products continue to dominate the consumer market while evidence-based treatments remain behind specialist-access walls.

The honest clinical picture in 2026: tinnitus distress is treatable. Loudness reduction remains rare. The tools are improving. Access to validated interventions is still limited by cost, geography, and the regulatory gap that keeps supplements on pharmacy shelves and Lenire out of reach for most sufferers.
The gap between what exists and who can get it isn't a research problem anymore.
What closes that gap — and who benefits from it staying open — is worth asking.
If you want the same investigator lens on anxiety and the natural-remedy evidence trail, that's the next transmission: anxiety natural remedies — what the trials show.
